title: Flawed Breadth-First Search (BFS) in a Graph

statement.markdown: Given a directed graph and a start node, your code should perform a breadth-first search (BFS) and return the visited nodes in the order they were visited. The graph is represented as an adjacency list.
statement.inputFormat: An adjacency list graph represented as a dictionary where each key is a node and its corresponding value is a list of its neighboring nodes. A start node to begin the BFS from.
statement.outputFormat: A list of nodes in the order they were visited during the BFS.
statement.notes: Note that the input graph will not contain self-loops or parallel edges. The start node will always be present in the graph.

examples.explanation: Start from node A and perform BFS

hints.text: Consider using a queue data structure to implement the BFS.

editorial.title: Breadth-First Search (BFS) in a Graph
editorial.intuition: BFS is a traversal algorithm that explores all the nodes at a given depth before moving to the next depth level.
editorial.bruteForce: A naive approach is to perform DFS and check if we've visited a node before.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a queue to keep track of nodes to visit.
editorial.optimalSolution: Perform BFS using a queue and a set to keep track of visited nodes.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: BFS visits all nodes at a given depth level before moving to the next depth level.
editorial.dryRun: Start from node A and perform BFS on the given graph.
commonMistakes: Forgot to add visited nodes to the result list

functionSignature.cpp: vector<string> solve(map<string, vector<string>>& graph, const string& startNode);
functionSignature.java: List<String> solve(Map<String, List<String>> graph, String startNode);
functionSignature.python: def solve(self, graph: dict, startNode: str) -> list:
